target.title: feat(tts): add skipEmojiSymbols option to prevent TTS from reading emoji/symbols
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds the opt-in messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols TTS preprocessing setting.
- Applies emoji/symbol stripping through the shared speech-core preparation path before file, stream, and telephony synthesis requests.
- Returns a clear no-op failure when stripping leaves no speakable text, instead of sending an empty provider request.
- Keeps default behavior unchanged when the option is disabled or unset.
- Reviewer focus: config schema/docs, the shared TTS preprocessing path, and the empty-after-strip guard.

Out of scope: changing default TTS behavior, validating every vendor-specific pronunciation path, or adding provider-specific filtering.

## Linked context

- Related: #77969.
- This was opened as a focused external fix for noisy TTS output when emoji and decorative symbols are spoken by providers.
- No maintainer-only deployment, credential, or production environment change is required.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: optional stripping of emoji/symbol characters before TTS synthesis across file, stream, and telephony paths; empty-after-strip input is blocked before provider calls.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, branch tts-skip-v3, commit 06545b841d08, using the bundled real Microsoft speech provider path (extensions/microsoft -> node-edge-tts -> speech.platform.bing.com). No API keys or private account secrets were used.
  - node --import tsx - <<'TS' ... register microsoft plugin, call textToSpeech twice with skipEmojiSymbols=false/true, then call an emoji-only enabled case ... TS
  - ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
  - node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts
  - pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
- Evidence after fix:

      $ node --import tsx - <<'TS'
      # Registers the bundled Microsoft Speech plugin and performs real synthesis
      # through node-edge-tts / speech.platform.bing.com after this patch.
      TS
      {
        "branch": "tts-skip-v3",
        "commit": "06545b841d08",
        "provider": "microsoft",
        "realProviderPath": "extensions/microsoft -> node-edge-tts -> speech.platform.bing.com",
        "inputText": "Proof phrase: package ready. Check mark ✓ copyright © melting face 🫠 done.",
        "observed": [
          {
            "name": "disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false",
            "skipEmojiSymbols": false,
            "success": true,
            "provider": "microsoft",
            "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
            "target": "audio-file",
            "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof/disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false.mp3",
            "bytes": 39456,
            "headerHex": "fff364c4"
          },
          {
            "name": "enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true",
            "skipEmojiSymbols": true,
            "success": true,
            "provider": "microsoft",
            "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
            "target": "audio-file",
            "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof/enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true.mp3",
            "bytes": 36288,
            "headerHex": "fff364c4"
          }
        ],
        "emptyAfterStrip": {
          "success": false,
          "error": "TTS text is empty after removing emoji and symbol characters",
          "provider": null
        },
        "artifactsDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof"
      }

      $ ffprobe -v error -show_entries format=duration,size ...
      ffprobe was not installed in this container, so artifact validation used byte size plus MP3 frame header checks.

      $ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
      Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests       32 passed (32)

      $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts
      Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests       14 passed (14)

      $ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
      exit_code=0

- Observed result after fix: disabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio from the original text containing ✓, ©, and 🫠; enabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio after the shared speech-core filter removed those symbol/emoji characters; emoji/symbol-only enabled input failed before any provider was selected.
- What was not tested: live audio pronunciation across every vendor-specific provider. The real-provider proof uses the bundled Microsoft speech provider because it is credential-free and exercises the same speech-core preprocessing path that all providers share.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: ffprobe was not installed in the proof container, so artifact validation used nonzero byte sizes and MP3 frame headers. The live proof used the credential-free Microsoft path rather than paid or account-bound providers.
- Before evidence: not captured in this PR body; the regression was that emoji/symbol text reached synthesis without a shared opt-in strip step and empty-after-strip requests could still reach provider setup paths.

## Tests and validation

Commands run for the behavioral change:

    node --import tsx - <<'TS' ...real Microsoft speech-provider proof...
    ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
    node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts
    pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod

Regression coverage added or updated:

- extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
- src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts

Locked-in scenarios:

- file synthesis strips complete emoji/symbol sequences when enabled.
- stream synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
- telephony synthesis receives stripped text when enabled.
- telephony synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
- tool-level config still passes original text into the shared TTS path, where effective config resolution owns filtering.

Template repair validation on 2026-05-27:

    Authenticated GitHub API read of the PR base template and PR body.
    Authenticated GitHub API update of this PR body.
    Authenticated GitHub API re-read verified these current template headings are present:
    Summary, Linked context, Real behavior proof (required for external PRs), Tests and validation, Risk checklist, Current review state.

## Risk checklist

- User-visible behavior changed: Yes, but only when messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols is explicitly enabled.
- Config, environment, or migration behavior changed: Yes, a new optional config field is added; no migration is needed and unset/false preserves existing behavior.
- Security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior changed: No.
- Highest-risk area: Unicode filtering might remove characters a user expected to hear, or filtering might diverge between TTS paths.
- Mitigation: the option is disabled by default, filtering is centralized in the shared speech-core preparation path, and regression tests cover file, stream, telephony, empty-after-strip, and adjacent tool behavior.

Compatibility notes:

- Backward compatible: Yes.
- Config/env changes: Yes, optional messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols.
- Migration needed: No.
- Upgrade steps: none unless a user wants to opt into emoji/symbol stripping.

## Current review state

- Next action: resolve the separate upstream/main generated config metadata conflicts for this branch, then let maintainers and automation continue review.
- Still waiting on author/maintainer/CI/external proof: author-side branch conflict refresh remains tracked separately; no additional external proof is being added in this body-only update.
- Bot or reviewer comments addressed: this body update repairs the current-template gap by replacing the older long-form PR body headings with every current upstream template heading and concrete content under each one.

target.title: fix #88009: [Feature]: batched memory embedding should batch over files
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #88009.

- Batch memory embedding work across dirty files when the provider explicitly opts into source-wide batch submission.
- Let OpenAI memory embedding batch jobs cover memory and session sources in one provider job while preserving per-file behavior for other batch runtimes.
- Make OpenAI batch polling more resilient with retryable status errors, progress counts, and exponential backoff.
- Document the source-wide batch runtime opt-in and cover its provider contract semantics.

## Rebase / CI unblocker

- The five hard Knip allowlist entries in `scripts/deadcode-unused-files.allowlist.mjs` are retained as a rebase/current-main CI unblocker, not as part of the memory batching feature scope.
- After merging current `main`, Linux `check-dependencies` reported these five agent cache/state DB files as `Unexpected unused files`; keeping them in the required allowlist preserves the exact CI contract and stale-entry guard.
- Maintainer acceptance is required if this PR carries that CI unblocker; otherwise these entries should be split into a separate tooling/CI PR before merge.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Memory indexing with OpenAI-compatible batch embeddings no longer submits one provider batch per memory or session file. Dirty memory files and session transcripts are prepared together, then submitted as one source-wide provider batch until file or request limits require a split.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux source checkout at `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-88009`, isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME` and `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-88009-evidence/real-behavior-proof`, local OpenAI-compatible batch endpoint on `127.0.0.1`, provider `openai`, model `text-embedding-3-small`, `remote.batch.enabled=true`, `remote.batch.wait=true`, `remote.batch.timeoutMinutes=1440`, `fallback=none`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-88009-evidence/run-real-behavior-proof.mjs
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-88009 && node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts extensions/openai/memory-embedding-adapter.test.ts extensions/openai/embedding-batch.test.ts
corepack pnpm@11.2.2 --dir /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-88009 tsgo:core
corepack pnpm@11.2.2 --dir /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-88009 tsgo:extensions
corepack pnpm@11.2.2 --dir /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-88009 exec oxlint extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-embedding-ops.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-ops.ts extensions/openai/embedding-batch.ts extensions/openai/memory-embedding-adapter.ts src/plugins/memory-embedding-providers.ts
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-88009 && node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/plugins/contracts/memory-embedding-provider.contract.test.ts
corepack pnpm@11.2.2 --dir /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-88009 tsgo:core
corepack pnpm@11.2.2 --dir /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-88009 exec oxlint src/plugins/contracts/memory-embedding-provider.contract.test.ts
git -C /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-88009 diff --check
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-88009 && HOME=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-88009-evidence/gateway-watch-proof/home OPENCLAW_HOME=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-88009-evidence/gateway-watch-proof/home OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-88009-evidence/gateway-watch-proof/state CI=1 timeout 90s corepack pnpm@11.2.2 gateway:watch:raw
```

- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
Real behavior proof for issue 88009
Exit code: 0
Fixture:
- memory files: 833
- session files: 495
- configured sources: memory,sessions
- remote.batch.enabled: true
- remote.batch.wait: true
- remote.batch.timeoutMinutes: 1440
- fallback: none

Mock provider observations:
- uploaded batch input files: 1
- provider batch jobs: 1
- requests submitted through provider batch jobs: 1328
- inline embedding requests: 0
- status poll sequence: 429/retryable, 200/in_progress, 200/completed

Relevant CLI output:
  [memory] embeddings: source-wide batch prepare files=1328 sources=memory=833,sessions=495 maxFiles=2048 maxRequests=50000
  [memory] embeddings: source-wide batch submit group=1 source=memory+sessions files=1328 chunks=1328 sources=memory=833,sessions=495 reason=end
  [memory] embeddings: openai batch submit
  [memory] embeddings: openai batch created
  [memory] openai batch batch-1 in_progress; progress 0/1328 failed=0; waiting 20ms
  [memory] openai batch batch-1 status check failed: openai batch status failed: 429 {"error":{"message":"local retryable status probe"}}; waiting 40ms
  [memory] openai batch batch-1 in_progress; progress 0/1328 failed=0; waiting 80ms
  Memory index updated (main).

Focused Vitest:
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 46.53s
extension-memory: 1 file passed, 28 tests passed
extension-provider-openai: 2 files passed, 4 tests passed

Type checks:
tsgo:core passed
tsgo:extensions passed

Targeted lint:
oxlint passed with no findings for the changed files

Follow-up contract coverage for provider API review:
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 18.76s
memory embedding provider contract: 1 file passed, 5 tests passed
tsgo:core passed
follow-up oxlint passed with no findings
git diff --check passed

Gateway watch startup path:
[gateway] loading configuration…
[gateway] force: no listeners on port 19891
[gateway] resolving authentication…
[gateway] auth mode=none explicitly configured; all gateway connections are unauthenticated.
[gateway] starting...
[gateway] starting HTTP server...
[gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: acpx, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 3.5s)
[gateway] ready
[gateway] signal SIGTERM received
[shutdown] completed cleanly in 470ms

Gateway watch failure-marker scan:
No INEFFECTIVE_DYNAMIC_IMPORT, Doctor repair failed, gateway:watch exiting, Build emitted, ELIFECYCLE, Error, or failed markers were present in gateway-watch-raw-configured.log.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The real `openclaw memory index --force --verbose` path submitted one OpenAI-compatible provider batch job for 1328 total chunks from 833 memory files and 495 session transcripts. The CLI log shows `source=memory+sessions`, `reason=end`, no inline embedding requests, progress counts during polling, retry of a 429 status check, and increasing waits from 20ms to 40ms to 80ms. The reporter's `gateway:watch:raw` startup path also reached `http server listening` and `gateway ready` under an isolated local gateway config, then shut down cleanly after the bounded watch proof timeout.
- **What was not tested:** Hosted OpenAI production batch service, non-Linux platforms, fixture sizes above the 50,000-request provider batch cap, authenticated gateway/channel traffic, and long-running gateway operation beyond the bounded startup proof window. The local endpoint implemented the OpenAI-compatible `/v1/files`, `/v1/batches`, `/v1/batches/:id`, and `/v1/files/:id/content` behavior needed for this indexing path.
- **Target test file:** `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts`, `extensions/openai/embedding-batch.test.ts`, `extensions/openai/memory-embedding-adapter.test.ts`, and `src/plugins/contracts/memory-embedding-provider.contract.test.ts`.

## Regression Test Plan

- Focused memory manager tests cover source-wide batching across dirty memory files, oversized-file chunk grouping, forced memory+session indexing, max-file/request bounds, and non-opt-in batch runtimes staying per-file.
- OpenAI embedding batch tests cover existing output parsing and batch behavior while the new polling path adds progress, retryable status failures, and backoff.
- Memory embedding provider contract tests cover the explicit source-wide batch runtime opt-in and returned embedding order semantics.
- Type checks, targeted lint, and `git diff --check` were run for the changed memory-core, OpenAI, provider runtime, and provider contract files.
- `gateway:watch:raw` was run with isolated local gateway state to verify the startup path reaches `gateway ready` without the prior dynamic-import/doctor-repair launch failure.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The sync layer indexed dirty memory files and session transcript files as separate file-level operations. Even when a provider exposed an async batch endpoint, each file reached `batchEmbed` independently, so a workload with many small files produced many small provider batch jobs. The OpenAI batch poll loop also treated transient status failures as terminal and used a fixed poll interval, which made long-running provider jobs noisy and brittle.

target.title: feat(telegram): opt-in interleaved progress lane
target.body:
## Summary

Add an opt-in Telegram renderer that projects reasoning text and structured runtime events into a single durable live message on the reasoning lane, matching CLI ergonomics without changing execution semantics.

**Gated on three conditions:** `toolProgressEnabled && interleavedProgress === true && hasReasoningLane` — inherits every existing group/DM/visibility gate.

## What it does

When `channels.telegram.streaming.preview.interleavedProgress` is enabled:
- Reasoning text streams into the interleaved message with a "Thinking" header
- Tool starts append a sanitized line (tool name only, never raw args by default)
- Item/plan/approval/command/patch events append title-only summary lines
- A rolling timer (20s cadence) shows elapsed time + a wall-clock stamp while running, so a stalled lane is visible at a glance; cleared on completion
- The model's reply is delivered once as the polished answer message — it is **not** mixed into the Thinking lane
- Long turns spill into continuation messages instead of freezing at Telegram's length cap
- Falls back to the existing `pushStreamToolProgress` lane when the flag is off

## Key design choices

- **Projection-only** — no changes to prompts, model routing, tool execution, or auth
- **No raw data** — tool lines show name only; `interleavedToolArgs` is a separate opt-in for sanitized args
- **No new event contract** — consumes existing `onReasoningStream`, `onToolStart`, `onItemEvent`, `onPlanUpdate`, `onApprovalEvent`, `onCommandOutput`, `onPatchSummary` callbacks unchanged
- **Reasoning + tools only; reply stays out** — the lane renders reasoning (`onReasoningStream`) and tool/event lines; the model's reply prose is **not** folded into it. Folding reply text in and stripping it back out at delivery proved unreliable — the model often drafts an answer, runs tools, then re-emits a revised one, leaving several answer-like copies of which only the last matches the polished final text. So the reply is delivered exactly once, as the answer. For the interactive backend the proxy restores real thinking on its own `onReasoningStream`, so reasoning is unaffected; a defensive final-answer strip remains for redacted-thinking backends that bridge assistant text into the reasoning lane. Tagged payloads (`<think>reason</think>answer`) keep only the in-tag reasoning, so answer prose after `</think>` never lands in the lane.
- **Single reasoning stream, delta-append** — appended as monotonic deltas (a cumulative snapshot contributes only its new suffix), so re-delivery never duplicates and the lane reads chronologically reasoning → tool → reasoning → …. The overlap fold is guarded: it folds a full-tail re-send (and full-increment duplicates) but never a short/mid-word coincidence, so a fresh fragment never loses its leading character.
- **Continuation on overflow** — as the durable message approaches Telegram's 4096-char cap the lane spills into a fresh continuation message (carrying recent context) instead of letting the draft stream stop editing and freeze the lane
- **Rate-limit-aware cadence** — the rolling "still running" timer ticks every 20s (not every few seconds) so N concurrently-streaming Thinking messages stay under Telegram's per-chat edit rate and don't draw 429s (a 429 drops the edit and stalls the lane); the line carries a wall-clock stamp so a genuine stall is obvious
- **No empty Thinking** — a turn that reaches final delivery with no reasoning/tool/status content (and no running timer) renders nothing, so the lane never emits a bare "Thinking" header
- **Plain-text transcript** — reasoning tag markers (`<think>` etc.) are unwrapped so the lane shows the model's prose, never raw tags

## Prerequisite

- **#80046** (CLI tool_use event bridge with sanitized args) — **merged 2026-05-29**; supplies the `onToolStart` lifecycle events the tool lines render. This branch should be rebased onto current `main` (which now contains it) before merge.

The renderer otherwise consumes only existing callbacks and is default-off, so it depends on no unmerged work. (On the standard `claude-cli` backend, CLI-*native* reasoning text additionally benefits from #85381's `thinking_delta`; without it the lane simply shows tool/event lines. The interactive backend restores thinking via its proxy, so reasoning appears regardless. Either way the default tool-progress lane is unaffected — this is not a hard dependency.)

## Changed files

- `extensions/telegram/src/interleaved-progress.ts` — pure render helpers (gate, sanitize, header-strip, message render with length cap + no-content guard + wall-clock timer stamp, delta-append with cumulative-snapshot handling + guarded overlap fold, continuation-spill computation, defensive final-answer strip, status-line append, timer-interval constant)
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts` — wire callbacks through the renderer with fallback; single reasoning-stream delta append (reply prose kept out of the lane); spill into a continuation message before the length cap; suppress an empty Thinking flush at final delivery; 20s rolling-timer cadence; reasoning-tag unwrapping; clear rolling timer in `finally`
- `extensions/telegram/src/reasoning-lane-coordinator.ts` — `stripReasoningTagsForInterleaved` (extract only in-`<think>` reasoning, dropping answer prose after `</think>`, preserving code regions)
- `src/config/types.telegram.ts` + `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts` — opt-in flag schema
- Config surface docs + bundled channel metadata regeneration

## Test plan

- [x] Unit tests: disabled-by-default, explicit opt-out, tool-progress-off, no-reasoning-lane, header dedup, rolling-timer lifecycle + wall-clock stamp, whitespace-collapse + length cap, delta-append no-duplication across a long cumulative stream, guarded overlap fold (no 1-char coincidence drop; full-tail re-send still folds), continuation-spill (repeated spills stay under the cap; giant-line force-advance; oversized-body tail-cap), no-content render guard, tagged-reasoning split (answer after `</think>` dropped; streaming/code cases), defensive final-answer strip
- [x] Live Telegram proof at current head (below)

## Verification

Local checks at the current head:

```
pnpm tsgo:extensions                                                 # typecheck — clean
pnpm exec oxlint extensions/telegram/src/interleaved-progress.ts \
                 extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts \
                 extensions/telegram/src/reasoning-lane-coordinator.ts   # lint — clean
npx vitest run extensions/telegram/src/interleaved-progress.test.ts \
               extensions/telegram/src/reasoning-lane-coordinator.test.ts   # all green
```

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Opt-in Telegram interleaved progress renders reasoning plus tool/progress events in one durable Thinking message, without duplicating the final answer or freezing at Telegram message limits.

Real environment tested: Real OpenClaw Telegram gateway against Telegram, default-off feature explicitly enabled, two concurrent Telegram threads in one chat.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Rebuilt and ran the gateway from the PR head, enabled channels.telegram.streaming.preview.interleavedProgress, sent concurrent Telegram prompts that produced reasoning, tool progress, long Thinking output, and idle tool waits, then inspected Telegram messages and the gateway outbound Telegram send log.

Evidence after fix: Screenshot embedded below plus redacted outbound Telegram send-log metrics copied below: continuation msg=34783 -> msg=34786, final answer absent from every Thinking edit, zero bare Thinking sends, 20s timer edits at 04:20:15 -> 04:20:35 -> 04:20:55, and 99 outbound calls with 0 failures.

Observed result after fix: Thinking lane showed reasoning plus tool line, rolled into a continuation before the cap, sent the polished answer once as its own message, cleared empty Thinking output, and avoided Telegram 429s under the two-thread concurrent run.

What was not tested: No broad channel matrix or release package lane in this PR; focused local proof covers the Telegram renderer helpers, schema, lint, and extension typecheck.

## Live proof (current head)

Captured on the current head with the default-off feature enabled, over a **two-thread concurrent** session. The screenshot shows the visible lane; the rest is verified from the gateway's **outbound Telegram send log** — every `sendMessage` / `editMessageText` the bot issues — which shows message-level behavior a screenshot can't (message ids, edit cadence, final-state content, timing). Wire-level facts below are message-level metrics (ids, lengths, ok/timing) and contain no command/path/host content.

**Normal interleaving — reasoning + tool-use line in the Thinking lane, with the rolling timer:**

![interleaved lane](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/66da4d16b5a9467a82a65134976bc66aa956519c/proof-assets/interleaved-progress-telegram-2026-05-30-toolline.png)

**Wire-level verification (gateway send log, current head, one two-thread concurrent session):**

- **Long-message continuation** — `msg=34783` grew to the 4000-char cap and rolled into a fresh continuation `msg=34786` (carrying the next tool line) instead of freezing; clean seam, no garble.
- **No duplicate final answer** — on that long thinking-heavy turn the final answer was delivered once as its own message and is **absent from every Thinking edit** of `34783`/`34786`.
- **No empty Thinking** — **zero** bare-`Thinking`-header sends across the session.
- **20s rolling-timer cadence (rate-limit relief)** — while idling on a blocking tool, the "still running" line re-edited at **exactly 20s intervals** (04:20:15 → 04:20:35 → 04:20:55, body static at 3764 chars), keeping concurrent Thinking messages under Telegram's edit rate. The wall-clock stamp exposes any genuine stall.
- **No rate-limiting** — **99** outbound calls across the two concurrent threads, **0** failures (no `429`s, no oversized-preview stop) — the exact concurrency that previously drew 429s.

target.title: Add operator install policy and remove dangerous-code install scanners
target.body:
## Summary

This PR adds operator-owned install policy enforcement for OpenClaw skills and plugins. Operators can configure a trusted `exec` policy command under `security.installPolicy`, and OpenClaw sends a structured request before supported install or update operations are allowed to proceed.

The policy applies to skill installs and plugin installs/updates from local paths, archives, Git, npm, ClawHub, gateway uploads, and managed or bundled paths where an install decision is made. ClawHub trust metadata is preserved as source context, but local operator policy is the final host-specific install gate.

This PR also removes the old install-time built-in dangerous-code scanner surface for both skills and plugins. `scan: false`, built-in dangerous-code install blocking, and force-unsafe bypass behavior are retired. The existing built-in plugin dependency denylist is preserved for compatibility and continues to block denied plugin dependencies before install. `--dangerously-force-unsafe-install` remains accepted on plugin install/update for compatibility, logs a deprecation warning, and is a no-op for dangerous-code scanning; it does not bypass operator install policy, plugin `before_install` hooks, or the plugin dependency denylist.

Diagnostic scanning remains available through `openclaw security audit --deep`, and plugin hook payloads keep an empty `builtinScan` compatibility field so existing hook handlers do not break on missing shape.

## Compatibility Notes

This PR intentionally rejects managed CLI installs of standalone plugin files through `openclaw plugins install <file>` and `openclaw plugins install --link <file>`. Evidence review found no credible user or docs reliance on that CLI single-file install path, and source inspection showed it was a shipped-but-broken/internal path: it could copy a file and report install success, but later plugin discovery expected an `openclaw.plugin.json` manifest at the plugin root and failed validation.

The documented standalone-file capability is preserved through `plugins.load.paths`. Managed local path installs now require plugin directories or archives with a manifest boundary; standalone plugin files should be configured explicitly in `plugins.load.paths` instead of installed as managed plugins.

## Schema Reference

### Configuration: `security.installPolicy`

- `enabled`: Enables operator-owned install policy. When `true`, OpenClaw requires a valid exec policy configuration and fails closed if the policy command cannot be run.
- `targets`: Optional array of target types to enforce. Supported values are `skill` and `plugin`; when omitted, every supported target type is enforced.
- `exec.source`: Transport discriminator. Current value is `exec`.
- `exec.command`: Absolute path to the trusted policy executable. It is launched without a shell.
- `exec.args`: Static argument list passed to the policy executable.
- `exec.timeoutMs`: Total wall-clock timeout for one policy decision.
- `exec.noOutputTimeoutMs`: Timeout for a policy process that produces no stdout or stderr.
- `exec.maxOutputBytes`: Maximum captured stdout/stderr bytes before failing closed.
- `exec.env`: Literal environment variables to provide to the policy process.
- `exec.passEnv`: Environment variable names copied from the OpenClaw process into the policy process.
- `exec.trustedDirs`: Optional trusted directory roots for policy executable validation.
- `exec.allowInsecurePath`: Allows a command path that would otherwise fail path ownership or permission hardening.
- `exec.allowSymlinkCommand`: Allows the configured command path to be a symlink; the resolved target is still validated.

### Policy stdin

OpenClaw writes one JSON object to policy stdin:

```json
{
  "protocolVersion": 1,
  "openclawVersion": "0.0.0",
  "targetType": "skill",
  "targetName": "example-skill",
  "sourcePath": "/tmp/openclaw-policy/example-skill",
  "sourcePathKind": "directory",
  "source": {
    "kind": "clawhub",
    "authority": "official",
    "mutable": false,
    "network": true
  },
  "origin": {
    "kind": "clawhub",
    "requestedSpecifier": "openclaw/example-skill"
  },
  "request": {
    "kind": "skill-install",
    "mode": "install",
    "requestedSpecifier": "openclaw/example-skill"
  },
  "skill": {
    "installId": "example-skill",
    "installSpec": {
      "kind": "node",
      "package": "@openclaw/example-skill",
      "bins": ["example-skill"]
    }
  }
}
```

- `protocolVersion`: Policy protocol version. Current value is `1`.
- `openclawVersion`: OpenClaw runtime version that produced the request.
- `targetType`: Install target family. Current values are `skill` and `plugin`.
- `targetName`: Resolved human-readable target name or id.
- `sourcePath`: Staged file or directory available for policy inspection.
- `sourcePathKind`: Shape of `sourcePath`; current values are `file` and `directory`.
- `source.kind`: Source class. Current values are `archive`, `bundled`, `clawhub`, `file`, `git`, `local-path`, `managed`, `npm`, `upload`, and `workspace`.
- `source.authority`: Source authority classification. Current values are `openclaw`, `official`, `third-party`, `unknown`, and `user`.
- `source.mutable`: Whether the referenced source can reasonably change without the requested specifier changing.
- `source.network`: Whether the source depends on network retrieval or remote authority.
- `origin`: Source-specific provenance details, such as local path, upload metadata, ClawHub entry, Git URL/ref, npm package, or plugin package details.
- `request.kind`: Entry-point classification. Current values are `skill-install`, `plugin-dir`, `plugin-archive`, `plugin-file`, `plugin-npm`, and `plugin-git`.
- `request.mode`: Requested operation mode. Current values are `install` and `update`.
- `request.requestedSpecifier`: Original requested specifier when available, preserving what the user, CLI, API, gateway, registry, or caller asked to install.
- `skill`: Present for skill targets. Includes `installId` and optional `installSpec`.
- `skill.installSpec`: Parsed skill install declaration, when declared by `SKILL.md`; supported kinds include `brew`, `node`, `go`, `uv`, and `download`.
- `plugin`: Present for plugin targets. Includes fields such as `pluginId`, `contentType`, `packageName`, `manifestId`, `version`, and archive/file extensions when available.

### Policy stdout

The policy command must write one JSON object to stdout:

```json
{
  "protocolVersion": 1,
  "decision": "block",
  "reason": "Policy requires review before installing remote plugins.",
  "findings": [
    {
      "ruleId": "remote-plugin-review",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "plugin.json",
      "line": 1,
      "message": "Remote plugin installs require operator approval."
    }
  ]
}
```

- `protocolVersion`: Must be `1`.
- `decision`: Required policy decision. Current values are `allow` and `block`.
- `reason`: Human-readable explanation. Required for `block`; recorded in install errors and minimal policy logging.
- `findings`: Optional structured policy findings for logs and diagnostics.
- `findings[].ruleId`: Stable policy rule identifier.
- `findings[].severity`: Finding severity. Current values are `info`, `warn`, and `critical`.
- `findings[].file`: Optional path within the inspected payload.
- `findings[].line`: Optional one-based line number.
- `findings[].message`: Human-readable finding message.
- `findings[].evidence`: Optional concise evidence string.

Policy failures are fail-closed. Non-zero exit, timeout, no-output timeout, malformed JSON, unsupported protocol version, missing required fields, or excessive output blocks the install.

## Verification

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/dependency-denylist.test.ts src/plugins/install.test.ts src/plugins/install.npm-spec.test.ts`
- Focused Vitest coverage for install policy, plugin install/update, skill install, ClawHub install, archive/path/npm/git installs, plugin CLI flag behavior, and plugin dependency denylist compatibility.
- Focused lint and formatting for touched install-policy, plugin, skill, CLI, gateway protocol, and docs surfaces.
- `pnpm build`
- `npm pack`
- `$autoreview`
- Changed-lane proof through Crabbox local-container.
- Local host packaged live E2E: full local skill/plugin/update/upload/restart matrix, live ClawHub skill/plugin allow and block, diagnostic audit, and active plugin hook interaction.
- Linux container packaged live E2E: full local skill/plugin/update/upload/restart matrix, live ClawHub skill/plugin allow and block, diagnostic audit, and active plugin hook interaction.
- Native Windows 11 Parallels packaged live E2E: local skill/plugin allow and block, malformed and timeout fail-closed behavior, scanner-removal regressions, deprecated unsafe flag warning, and policy source-path availability.
- Docker Telegram live E2E using Convex-leased `telegram` credentials.
- Upgrade/residue live E2E on local host and Linux container: latest published npm release upgraded to the candidate package, stale scanner config rejected before repair, `doctor --fix` removed it, `config validate` passed afterward, and post-upgrade skill install, plugin install, plugin update block, fail-closed, and recovery behavior worked.
- Plugin dependency denylist live compatibility proof: package-installed candidate CLI blocked denied plugin dependencies with no operator policy, with an allow policy, with deprecated unsafe flag, and with vendored `node_modules`; a dangerous-code-only plugin without denied dependencies installed successfully.
- Plugin dependency denylist live upgrade proof: latest published npm package blocked the denied dependency first, then the candidate package installed over it and preserved the dependency block across no-policy, allow-policy, deprecated unsafe-flag, and vendored dependency cases.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Operators can enforce local install policy for skills and plugins, including ClawHub-sourced installs, while install-time built-in dangerous-code scanner blocking is removed in favor of explicit operator policy and diagnostic audit. Existing plugin dependency-denylist blocking is preserved.

Real environment tested: Isolated packaged installs on local host, Linux container, native Windows 11 through Parallels, broad changed-lane proof through Crabbox local-container, Docker Telegram live E2E with Convex-leased `telegram` credentials, upgrade-from-published-package proofs on local host and Linux container, and dedicated plugin dependency-denylist compatibility/upgrade proofs.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: The verification commands and live package scenarios listed above were run against this branch and the generated package candidate.

Evidence after fix: Local, Linux, Windows, Telegram, upgrade/residue, plugin dependency-denylist compatibility, focused unit, build, lint, and changed-lane proofs completed successfully.

Observed result after fix: Supported skill and plugin install/update paths were policy-gated when install policy was enabled, policy source paths were available to the policy process, malformed/timed-out/unavailable policy responses failed closed, deprecated scanner residue was cleaned by doctor, dangerous-looking plugin/skill payloads installed when operator policy allowed the operation, and plugins with denied dependencies remained blocked before install.

What was not tested: Full release publishing and public release promotion flows.

target.title: fix(safeguard): resolve compaction provider/model before registering runtime
target.body:
Fixes #57901.

## Summary

Two-part fix for the safeguard auto-compaction path ignoring `agents.defaults.compaction.model`:

1. **`extensions.ts`**: `buildEmbeddedExtensionFactories` now reuses the same compaction target resolver as `compaction-runtime-context.ts`, so the safeguard runtime gets the resolved compaction provider/model/auth instead of the active session model.
2. **`compaction-safeguard.ts`**: swaps precedence in the hook from `ctx.model ?? runtime?.model` to `runtime?.model ?? ctx.model`. Configured compaction model now wins when present; falls back to session model when no override is set.

## Why the previous PR (#57903) wasn't enough

Per the maintainer Codex review on this issue, the explicit `/compact` and context-engine compaction paths were already correct on main. The closed PR #57903 only swapped the precedence in the hook. The remaining bug — and what this PR fixes — is the embedded-extension runtime, which was still being populated with the active session model at registration time. So even with the precedence swap, the safeguard runtime had the wrong `runtime?.model` to compare against.

## Tests

**769 tests pass, 0 errors, 0 failed.**

New regression: `extensions.test.ts` and `compaction-safeguard.test.ts` cover the case where `ctx.model` = session model (e.g., Opus) and `agents.defaults.compaction.model` = different (e.g., Sonnet). Asserts the configured compaction model wins.

Maintainer review bar (per [comment 4336639752](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/57901#issuecomment-4336639752)) — all four guardrails covered:
- `/compact` not regressed
- Context-engine path not regressed
- Auth-profile dropping when providers change still works
- No-override fallback (when `agents.defaults.compaction.model` is unset) still falls back to session model

## Stats

- 7 files, +202 / -9
- CHANGELOG.md updated

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Safeguard auto-compaction was ignoring `agents.defaults.compaction.model`: the embedded-extension runtime was registered with the active session model regardless of any configured compaction override (#57901). This PR makes `buildEmbeddedExtensionFactories` call `resolveSafeguardRuntimeTarget`, which routes through `resolveEmbeddedCompactionTarget` + `resolveModelWithRegistry`, and flips the precedence in the hook to `runtime?.model ?? ctx.model`. Inline-only models defined under `models.providers.*.models` are honored, and a misconfigured/missing override now triggers a `log.warn(...)` instead of silently registering `model: undefined`.

**Real environment tested:** exe.dev VM, `openclaw/openclaw` fork `joeykrug/openclaw`, worktree at `.../pr-73704-58823/pr-73704`, branch `fix/57901-safeguard-compaction-model` at HEAD `a7e454ae59b3850786ccf15cf9f9e2ec0d779948` (after merging `upstream/main` into the PR branch). Node `v24.15.0`, pnpm `11.1.0`. Sources read live from `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extensions.ts`, `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-context.ts`, `src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard-runtime.ts`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

1. `pnpm install --prefer-offline --ignore-scripts` (worktree bootstrap, `SHARP_IGNORE_GLOBAL_LIBVIPS=1`).
2. Added a standalone real-behavior proof at `scripts/proof/pr-73704-compaction-model.mjs` that imports the actual patched source — `buildEmbeddedExtensionFactories`, `resolveSafeguardRuntimeTarget`, `resolveEmbeddedCompactionTarget`, and `getCompactionSafeguardRuntime` — and exercises three scenarios end-to-end (no vitest, no mocks of the function under test).
3. Ran: `node --import tsx scripts/proof/pr-73704-compaction-model.mjs` from the worktree root. Captured terminal output verbatim below.
4. Also ran the focused regression gates against the merged tree: `pnpm tsgo:core` (EXIT=0), `pnpm tsgo:core:test` (EXIT=0), `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extensions.test.ts src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.auth-profile-propagation.test.ts` (Tests: 99 passed across 3 files), `pnpm lint:core` (0 warnings, 0 errors).

**Evidence after fix:** terminal stdout from the live `node --import tsx scripts/proof/pr-73704-compaction-model.mjs` run against the patched source (exit code 0):

```text
$ node --import tsx scripts/proof/pr-73704-compaction-model.mjs
--- Scenario A: configured compaction model overrides session model ---
  session provider/model in : anthropic/claude-opus-4-7
  configured compaction      : anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 (from agents.defaults.compaction.model)
  resolveEmbeddedCompactionTarget => provider="anthropic" model="claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   pure target provider = "anthropic"
  OK   pure target model = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
    modelRegistry.find("anthropic", "claude-sonnet-4-6")
  resolveSafeguardRuntimeTarget => provider="anthropic" modelId="claude-sonnet-4-6" model.id="claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   runtime target provider = "anthropic"
  OK   runtime target modelId = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   runtime target model.id = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   runtime target model.provider = "anthropic"
  OK   runtime target model.id is NOT session id = true
    modelRegistry.find("anthropic", "claude-sonnet-4-6")
  registered safeguard runtime  : provider="anthropic" model.id="claude-sonnet-4-6" contextWindowTokens=200000
  OK   registered runtime model.id = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   registered runtime model.provider = "anthropic"
  OK   registered runtime model.id != session model id = true

--- Scenario B: no compaction.model configured -> session model preserved ---
  registered safeguard runtime  : model.id="claude-opus-4-7" (session id was "claude-opus-4-7")
  OK   session model.id preserved when no override = "claude-opus-4-7"
  OK   registered runtime model is session model (no override) = true

--- Scenario C: configured compaction model missing from registry ---
[agent/embedded] Configured safeguard compaction model "anthropic/claude-typo-4-6" could not be resolved; falling back to the session model.
  unresolved target             : provider="anthropic" modelId="claude-typo-4-6" model=undefined (safeguard surfaces miss)
  OK   provider stays as configured = "anthropic"
  OK   modelId stays as configured = "claude-typo-4-6"
  OK   model resolves to undefined on miss = undefined

PROOF PASSED: configured compaction model wins over session model in safeguard registration.
```

Supplementary CI gates against the merged tree (all green):

```text
$ pnpm tsgo:core
EXIT=0  (no diagnostics)

$ pnpm tsgo:core:test
EXIT=0  (no diagnostics)

$ pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extensions.test.ts src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.auth-profile-propagation.test.ts
 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  99 passed (99)

$ pnpm lint:core
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
```

**Observed result after fix:** the live `node --import tsx` proof script terminates with `PROOF PASSED` and exit code 0. Scenario A: with `agents.defaults.compaction.model = "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6"` and session model `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, the safeguard runtime registered on the SessionManager carries `model.id = "claude-sonnet-4-6"` and `model.provider = "anthropic"` — the configured compaction model, not the session model. Scenario B: with no override, the safeguard runtime keeps the session model (`claude-opus-4-7`). Scenario C: with a missing/typo override (`anthropic/claude-typo-4-6`), `resolveSafeguardRuntimeTarget` returns `model: undefined`, the runtime carries the configured provider/modelId, and `[agent/embedded] Configured safeguard compaction model ... could not be resolved; falling back to the session model.` is emitted on stderr/stdout. Behavior matches the precedence inverted in `compaction-safeguard.ts` (`const model = runtime?.model ?? ctx.model;`).

**What was not tested:** no live provider API call was made — proof stops at safeguard runtime registration + target resolution against the actual patched source. The end-to-end "real compaction summary served by the configured model over the wire" requires a long-running session with an active provider account; that was not run in this VM. The script also does not exercise the `extensions.test.ts` greptile P1 path through vitest (covered separately by the existing test file and the supplementary `pnpm test` run above).

## Summary

- Continue the inline-comment pass after PR #88554 on current `main`.
- Document the auth-profile failure policy boundary that decides which failover reasons affect shared provider health.
- Document heartbeat-only trigger policy prompt injection and the default non-heartbeat prompt shape.
- Document embedded model fallback override precedence, including explicit empty override behavior.
- Document RuntimePlan-first transcript policy resolution and legacy provider-runtime fallback adaptation.
- Document embedded abort-settle cleanup timeout resolution and strict decimal env override behavior.
- Document embedded attempt run decisions for write-lock timing, forwarded auth-profile provenance, unknown-tool guarding, hook gating, and tool-policy provider fallback.
- Document the narrow replay-safe Codex app-server recovery retry gate for client-close and completion-idle failures.
- Keep the change comment-only: no file header and no import-block comment.

## Verification

Behavior addressed: comment-only follow-up for auth-profile failover health policy, heartbeat trigger prompt policy, embedded model fallback override policy, attempt transcript policy selection, abort-settle timeout resolution, embedded run-decision contracts, and Codex app-server recovery retry policy; no runtime behavior intended.
Real environment tested: local source checkout.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: git diff --check; git diff --check origin/main..HEAD; pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/auth-profile-failure-policy.test.ts; pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/trigger-policy.test.ts; pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/fallbacks.test.ts; pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.transcript-policy.test.ts; pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.abort-settle-timeout.test.ts; pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.run-decisions.test.ts; pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.codex-app-server-recovery.test.ts.

target.title: fix(memory): warn on async runtime watcher pressure

## Summary

- re-add the memory-watch FD pressure warning after #89246 reverted the earlier warning-only PR
- keep memory watching enabled by default and warn only when runtime watcher state is already large enough to be risky
- move the warning out of config validation so normal startup config loading does not recursively scan memory folders
- cover built-in memory watching, QMD collection watching, and the existing-chokidar native-fallback path with focused watcher tests

## ClawSweeper feedback addressed

- fixed the missed warning path when a native directory watcher fails at runtime and the large memory directory is added to an existing chokidar watcher
- deferred and bounded the fallback pressure check so chokidar has time to populate `getWatched()` after async `add()` work
- clear the deferred pressure-check timer during manager shutdown

## Related

- Replaces the warning behavior reverted by #89246.
- Follow-up to #89185, but now self-contained because #89185 was reverted.

target.title: Add operator install policy and remove dangerous-code install scanners
target.body:
## Summary

This PR adds operator-owned install policy enforcement for OpenClaw skills and plugins. Operators can configure a trusted `exec` policy command under `security.installPolicy`, and OpenClaw sends a structured request before supported install or update operations are allowed to proceed.

The policy applies to skill installs and plugin installs/updates from local paths, archives, Git, npm, ClawHub, gateway uploads, and managed or bundled paths where an install decision is made. ClawHub trust metadata is preserved as source context, but local operator policy is the final host-specific install gate.

This PR also removes the old install-time built-in dangerous-code scanner surface for both skills and plugins. `scan: false`, built-in dangerous-code install blocking, and force-unsafe bypass behavior are retired. The existing built-in plugin dependency denylist is preserved for compatibility and continues to block denied plugin dependencies before install. `--dangerously-force-unsafe-install` remains accepted on plugin install/update for compatibility, logs a deprecation warning, and is a no-op for dangerous-code scanning; it does not bypass operator install policy, plugin `before_install` hooks, or the plugin dependency denylist.

Diagnostic scanning remains available through `openclaw security audit --deep`, and plugin hook payloads keep an empty `builtinScan` compatibility field so existing hook handlers do not break on missing shape.

## Compatibility Notes

This PR intentionally rejects managed CLI installs of standalone plugin files through `openclaw plugins install <file>` and `openclaw plugins install --link <file>`. Evidence review found no credible user or docs reliance on that CLI single-file install path, and source inspection showed it was a shipped-but-broken/internal path: it could copy a file and report install success, but later plugin discovery expected an `openclaw.plugin.json` manifest at the plugin root and failed validation.

The documented standalone-file capability is preserved through `plugins.load.paths`. Managed local path installs now require plugin directories or archives with a manifest boundary; standalone plugin files should be configured explicitly in `plugins.load.paths` instead of installed as managed plugins.

## Schema Reference

### Configuration: `security.installPolicy`

- `enabled`: Enables operator-owned install policy. When `true`, OpenClaw requires a valid exec policy configuration and fails closed if the policy command cannot be run.
- `targets`: Optional array of target types to enforce. Supported values are `skill` and `plugin`; when omitted, every supported target type is enforced.
- `exec.source`: Transport discriminator. Current value is `exec`.
- `exec.command`: Absolute path to the trusted policy executable. It is launched without a shell.
- `exec.args`: Static argument list passed to the policy executable.
- `exec.timeoutMs`: Total wall-clock timeout for one policy decision.
- `exec.noOutputTimeoutMs`: Timeout for a policy process that produces no stdout or stderr.
- `exec.maxOutputBytes`: Maximum captured stdout/stderr bytes before failing closed.
- `exec.env`: Literal environment variables to provide to the policy process.
- `exec.passEnv`: Environment variable names copied from the OpenClaw process into the policy process.
- `exec.trustedDirs`: Optional trusted directory roots for policy executable validation.
- `exec.allowInsecurePath`: Allows a command path that would otherwise fail path ownership or permission hardening.
- `exec.allowSymlinkCommand`: Allows the configured command path to be a symlink; the resolved target is still validated.

### Policy stdin

OpenClaw writes one JSON object to policy stdin:

```json
{
  "protocolVersion": 1,
  "openclawVersion": "0.0.0",
  "targetType": "skill",
  "targetName": "example-skill",
  "sourcePath": "/tmp/openclaw-policy/example-skill",
  "sourcePathKind": "directory",
  "source": {
    "kind": "clawhub",
    "authority": "official",
    "mutable": false,
    "network": true
  },
  "origin": {
    "kind": "clawhub",
    "requestedSpecifier": "openclaw/example-skill"
  },
  "request": {
    "kind": "skill-install",
    "mode": "install",
    "requestedSpecifier": "openclaw/example-skill"
  },
  "skill": {
    "installId": "example-skill",
    "installSpec": {
      "kind": "node",
      "package": "@openclaw/example-skill",
      "bins": ["example-skill"]
    }
  }
}
```

- `protocolVersion`: Policy protocol version. Current value is `1`.
- `openclawVersion`: OpenClaw runtime version that produced the request.
- `targetType`: Install target family. Current values are `skill` and `plugin`.
- `targetName`: Resolved human-readable target name or id.
- `sourcePath`: Staged file or directory available for policy inspection.
- `sourcePathKind`: Shape of `sourcePath`; current values are `file` and `directory`.
- `source.kind`: Source class. Current values are `archive`, `bundled`, `clawhub`, `file`, `git`, `local-path`, `managed`, `npm`, `upload`, and `workspace`.
- `source.authority`: Source authority classification. Current values are `openclaw`, `official`, `third-party`, `unknown`, and `user`.
- `source.mutable`: Whether the referenced source can reasonably change without the requested specifier changing.
- `source.network`: Whether the source depends on network retrieval or remote authority.
- `origin`: Source-specific provenance details, such as local path, upload metadata, ClawHub entry, Git URL/ref, npm package, or plugin package details.
- `request.kind`: Entry-point classification. Current values are `skill-install`, `plugin-dir`, `plugin-archive`, `plugin-file`, `plugin-npm`, and `plugin-git`.
- `request.mode`: Requested operation mode. Current values are `install` and `update`.
- `request.requestedSpecifier`: Original requested specifier when available, preserving what the user, CLI, API, gateway, registry, or caller asked to install.
- `skill`: Present for skill targets. Includes `installId` and optional `installSpec`.
- `skill.installSpec`: Parsed skill install declaration, when declared by `SKILL.md`; supported kinds include `brew`, `node`, `go`, `uv`, and `download`.
- `plugin`: Present for plugin targets. Includes fields such as `pluginId`, `contentType`, `packageName`, `manifestId`, `version`, and archive/file extensions when available.

### Policy stdout

The policy command must write one JSON object to stdout:

```json
{
  "protocolVersion": 1,
  "decision": "block",
  "reason": "Policy requires review before installing remote plugins.",
  "findings": [
    {
      "ruleId": "remote-plugin-review",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "plugin.json",
      "line": 1,
      "message": "Remote plugin installs require operator approval."
    }
  ]
}
```

- `protocolVersion`: Must be `1`.
- `decision`: Required policy decision. Current values are `allow` and `block`.
- `reason`: Human-readable explanation. Required for `block`; recorded in install errors and minimal policy logging.
- `findings`: Optional structured policy findings for logs and diagnostics.
- `findings[].ruleId`: Stable policy rule identifier.
- `findings[].severity`: Finding severity. Current values are `info`, `warn`, and `critical`.
- `findings[].file`: Optional path within the inspected payload.
- `findings[].line`: Optional one-based line number.
- `findings[].message`: Human-readable finding message.
- `findings[].evidence`: Optional concise evidence string.

Policy failures are fail-closed. Non-zero exit, timeout, no-output timeout, malformed JSON, unsupported protocol version, missing required fields, or excessive output blocks the install.

## Verification

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/dependency-denylist.test.ts src/plugins/install.test.ts src/plugins/install.npm-spec.test.ts`
- Focused Vitest coverage for install policy, plugin install/update, skill install, ClawHub install, archive/path/npm/git installs, plugin CLI flag behavior, and plugin dependency denylist compatibility.
- Focused lint and formatting for touched install-policy, plugin, skill, CLI, gateway protocol, and docs surfaces.
- `pnpm build`
- `npm pack`
- `$autoreview`
- Changed-lane proof through Crabbox local-container.
- Local host packaged live E2E: full local skill/plugin/update/upload/restart matrix, live ClawHub skill/plugin allow and block, diagnostic audit, and active plugin hook interaction.
- Linux container packaged live E2E: full local skill/plugin/update/upload/restart matrix, live ClawHub skill/plugin allow and block, diagnostic audit, and active plugin hook interaction.
- Native Windows 11 Parallels packaged live E2E: local skill/plugin allow and block, malformed and timeout fail-closed behavior, scanner-removal regressions, deprecated unsafe flag warning, and policy source-path availability.
- Docker Telegram live E2E using Convex-leased `telegram` credentials.
- Upgrade/residue live E2E on local host and Linux container: latest published npm release upgraded to the candidate package, stale scanner config rejected before repair, `doctor --fix` removed it, `config validate` passed afterward, and post-upgrade skill install, plugin install, plugin update block, fail-closed, and recovery behavior worked.
- Plugin dependency denylist live compatibility proof: package-installed candidate CLI blocked denied plugin dependencies with no operator policy, with an allow policy, with deprecated unsafe flag, and with vendored `node_modules`; a dangerous-code-only plugin without denied dependencies installed successfully.
- Plugin dependency denylist live upgrade proof: latest published npm package blocked the denied dependency first, then the candidate package installed over it and preserved the dependency block across no-policy, allow-policy, deprecated unsafe-flag, and vendored dependency cases.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Operators can enforce local install policy for skills and plugins, including ClawHub-sourced installs, while install-time built-in dangerous-code scanner blocking is removed in favor of explicit operator policy and diagnostic audit. Existing plugin dependency-denylist blocking is preserved.

Real environment tested: Isolated packaged installs on local host, Linux container, native Windows 11 through Parallels, broad changed-lane proof through Crabbox local-container, Docker Telegram live E2E with Convex-leased `telegram` credentials, upgrade-from-published-package proofs on local host and Linux container, and dedicated plugin dependency-denylist compatibility/upgrade proofs.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: The verification commands and live package scenarios listed above were run against this branch and the generated package candidate.

Evidence after fix: Local, Linux, Windows, Telegram, upgrade/residue, plugin dependency-denylist compatibility, focused unit, build, lint, and changed-lane proofs completed successfully.

Observed result after fix: Supported skill and plugin install/update paths were policy-gated when install policy was enabled, policy source paths were available to the policy process, malformed/timed-out/unavailable policy responses failed closed, deprecated scanner residue was cleaned by doctor, dangerous-looking plugin/skill payloads installed when operator policy allowed the operation, and plugins with denied dependencies remained blocked before install.

What was not tested: Full release publishing and public release promotion flows.

target.title: fix(agents): reclaim session write-locks held past the holder's own maxHoldMs
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: Issue #87483 reports that a session write-lock file held by a **live** gateway process persists for hours past `maxHoldMs`/`staleMs`; the watchdog never reclaims it, so later requests fail with `session file locked (timeout 60000ms): pid=…`. The reporter's holder process was alive and busy (83% CPU) while holding the lock for 8+ hours despite the lock recording `maxHoldMs: 1020000` (17 min).
- **Root Cause**: There are two reclaim mechanisms and both fail for a live-but-stuck holder:
  1. The per-process watchdog (`ensureWatchdogStarted`) is a `setInterval` running **inside the holding process**. If that process is stuck in a synchronous CPU-bound loop, its event loop is blocked and the watchdog timer never fires, so the holder never self-releases.
  2. A contending process inspects the lock and, at `acquireSessionWriteLock` time (with `respectMaxHold` on), correctly flags it `hold-exceeded` (age past the holder's own recorded `maxHoldMs`). But `shouldRemoveContendedLockFile` (the `shouldRemoveStaleLock` callback the lock library consults under `staleRecovery: "remove-if-unchanged"`) treats **both** `too-old` and `hold-exceeded` as report-only: `REPORT_ONLY_STALE_LOCK_REASONS = {"too-old", "hold-exceeded"}`. When a live holder's only stale reasons are these, the function returns `false` (don't remove), the library throws `file_lock_stale`, and the acquire eventually fails — the lock is never reclaimed even though it is past the holder's own declared max hold. So a live-but-stuck holder pins the lock forever: it cannot self-release (event loop blocked) and contenders are forbidden from reclaiming it.
- **Fix**: Remove `"hold-exceeded"` from `REPORT_ONLY_STALE_LOCK_REASONS`. A holder past its **own recorded `maxHoldMs`** is overdue by its own contract and is reclaimable by a contender. `"too-old"` (past the global `staleMs` heuristic) stays report-only — a live holder may still be working within its own declared `maxHoldMs`, so we do not steal its lock on the global heuristic alone. The acquire path's `remove-if-unchanged` recovery still skips a lock whose file has changed (e.g. a concurrent release), so only a genuinely stalled holder loses the lock.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/agents/session-write-lock.ts` — drop `"hold-exceeded"` from `REPORT_ONLY_STALE_LOCK_REASONS` (one element + an explanatory comment).
  - `src/agents/session-write-lock.test.ts` — add an integration regression: a live OpenClaw-owned lock past its own `maxHoldMs` (but within `staleMs`) is now reclaimed by `acquireSessionWriteLock`.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - `"too-old"` stays report-only, so a live holder past the global `staleMs` but within its own `maxHoldMs` is still respected (covered by the existing "reports live OpenClaw-owned stale locks without removing them" test).
  - The `cleanStaleLockFiles` doctor sweep does not pass `respectMaxHold` and therefore never produces `hold-exceeded`; its behavior is unchanged (it still force-removes only dead/recycled/orphan locks, never a live holder's).
  - No config surface (no schema, defaults, env var, doctor migration, or `docs/reference/config`), no plugin SDK surface, no dependency changes. The watchdog interval, timeout, `staleMs`, and `maxHoldMs` resolution are untouched.

### Reproduction

1. A long-running gateway holds a session write-lock and gets stuck in a synchronous CPU loop (event loop blocked).
2. The holder's in-process watchdog can't fire, so the lock is never self-released past `maxHoldMs`.
3. Another request tries to write the same session; it sees the lock past the holder's `maxHoldMs` but cannot reclaim it.
4. **Before this PR**: the contender fails with a stale/`session file locked` error; the lock persists until the gateway restarts or the file is deleted manually.
5. **After this PR**: the contender reclaims the lock (the stalled holder's overdue lock is removed-if-unchanged) and proceeds.

Deterministic source reproduction (added as a regression test): a live OpenClaw-owned lock file with `maxHoldMs: 1000` and `createdAt` 30s ago, acquired with a large `staleMs`. Before the fix the acquire throws `SessionWriteLockStaleError: session file lock stale (hold-exceeded): … alive=true`; after the fix it reclaims the lock.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#87483 — a stalled live holder's lock is reclaimable)**: a session write-lock held past the holder's own `maxHoldMs` by a live OpenClaw process is reclaimed by a contender instead of pinning the session indefinitely.

**Real environment tested (Linux, Node — Vitest against the production `acquireSessionWriteLock` with a real spawned OpenClaw-recognized owner process; no live gateway)**: verified via `session-write-lock.test.ts`, plus core type-check, lint, and format on the changed files. A live overnight gateway lockup was not reproduced.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch (repo-root)**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/session-write-lock.test.ts`; `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json`; `oxfmt --check` and `oxlint` on the changed files.

**Evidence after fix (Vitest output for the touched test file)**:

```text
 session-write-lock.test.ts   Tests  54 passed (54)
```

The new case fails on current `main` exactly as the issue describes — `SessionWriteLockStaleError: session file lock stale (hold-exceeded): pid=… alive=true ageMs=30005` — and passes after dropping `"hold-exceeded"` from the report-only set, while all 53 existing cases (including the conservative "report live too-old locks without removing them") stay green.

**Observed result after fix (overdue live holder reclaimed; conservative live holder respected)**: a live holder past its own `maxHoldMs` is reclaimed; a live holder merely past the global `staleMs` (within its `maxHoldMs`) is still only reported, not removed.

**What was not tested (live overnight gateway lockup)**: the multi-hour real-gateway lock retention was not reproduced live; the proof is the deterministic module-level reproduction.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: reclaiming a live holder's lock could race with a holder that is making progress. **Mitigation**: only `hold-exceeded` (past the holder's **own** recorded `maxHoldMs`) becomes reclaimable, and the acquire path uses `remove-if-unchanged` — a holder whose lock file has changed (e.g. a concurrent release) is skipped; only a stalled holder (static lock past its own max hold) loses it.
- **Risk**: behavior change in session-state concurrency. **Mitigation**: the change narrows report-only to the global `staleMs` heuristic; it does not touch thresholds, the watchdog, or the doctor sweep, and the conservative live-`too-old` behavior is preserved (covered by an existing test).

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Agents / session write-lock

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #87483

PR surface: Source +62, Tests +287, Docs +1. Total +350 across 3 files.

Reproducibility: yes. The PR body supplies after-fix Gateway logs for the activation path, and source inspection shows current main still has the hot-mode ignore branch; I did not rerun the live Gateway scenario in this read-only review.
